Now join the legs and place both hands beside the body, palms facing downwards. Inhale and lift both the legs off the floor without bending the knees. Maintain the final position for three to five breaths with body awareness. Exhale and bring the legs down to the floor. Relax in macarasan. Supine postures situhasan. The bridge posture. Caution. If you have stomach ulcers or hernia, it is not recommended to practice this asan. Lie down on the ground. Stretch out your legs comfortably and place the palms facing downwards. Bend both the legs at the knees and bring the heels near the buttocks while holding both the ankles as per your convenience. Inhale slowly raise your buttocks and trunk up as much as you can to form a bridge and maintain the final position for three to five breaths with body awareness. Exhale. Slowly bring your hips down and straighten the legs in supine position. Relax. Utan padasan. The raised leg posture. Caution. Individuals with hypertension should practice this by alternating between legs. Lie comfortably on the ground with legs stretched out. Hands placed by the sides and palms facing down. While inhaling, slowly raise both legs without bending them at the knees until you are able to see the tips of toes. Maintain the final position for three to five breaths with body awareness. Exhale slowly. Bring both legs down and place them on the ground. Relax. Arda halasan. The half plow posture. Caution. Avoid this practice in case of abdominal injuries, hernia or lumbosacral pains. Lie comfortably on the ground with legs stretched out. Keep hands placed by the side and palms facing down. Inhale slowly raise both the legs without bending them at the knees and bring them vertical to the ground. The body from hip to shoulder should be kept straight. Maintain the final position for three to five breaths with body awareness. Exhale slowly. Bring the legs down to the ground without lifting the head. Relax. Pavan muktasan. The wind-freeing posture. Caution. Avoid this practice in case of abdominal injuries, hernia, sciatica or severe back pain. Lie comfortably on the ground with legs stretched out, hands placed by the sides and palms facing downwards. Bend both the knees. Exhale. Bring the knees towards the chest. Inhale. Interlock the fingers and clasp the shin below knees. Exhale. Raise the head up until the chin or nose touches the knees. Maintain the final position for three to five breaths with body awareness. Inhale. Bring the head back to the ground. Exhale. Release the legs back to the floor. Lie down in supine position. [Music] Relax. [Music] Shavasan, the corpse posture. Lie down on the ground. Open the feet apart with toes pointing sideways and palms facing upwards. Close your eyes. Consciously relax the whole body. Become aware of the natural flow of breath. It will tend to become slow. Remain in the position until you feel refreshed and relaxed. [Music] While coming back, slowly stretch your arm over the head and join the feet. Then turn by the side either right or left and come back to the sitting position with the support of the hands. [Music] Kapal bhhati. Caution. Avoid this practice in case of cardiac conditions, epilepsy, hernia, gastric ulcer or during menstrual cycle. Sit in any comfortable position. Keep the spine and head straight with eyes closed. Place the hands on the knees in ghan mudra. Inhale deeply through both the nostrils till you start to experience fullness in the pelvic region and expansion in the chest. Expel the breath with forceful contractions of the lower abdominal muscles and let the inhalation be passive. Let us begin the first round of kapal bhhati with 20 strokes. [Music] Exhale deeply and relax. This is one round of kapal bhhati. Repeat two more rounds with 20 strokes each. [Music] Nari shodhan praarayam the alternate nostril breathing start with sitting in any comfortable posture. Breath should be slow, steady and controlled. Keep spine and head straight with the eyes closed. Relax the body with few deep breaths. Keep the left palm on the left knee in gan mudra and the right hand should be in pranayam mudra with the middle and the index fingers folded like this. Close the right nostril with the right thumb and exhale completely. Now inhale through the left nostril. Recognize the touch of air moving through your body as pra. Then close the left nostril with the ring and little finger of the right hand and exhale through the right nostril. Now inhale through the right nostril. Close the right nostril with the thumb and exhale through the left nostril. This is one round of nari shodhan praam. Repeat it for four more rounds.  pranayam. Caution. People suffering from cough, cold or tonsillitis should avoid the practice of this pranayam. Sit in any comfortable posture. Keep the spine and head straight with eyes closed. Place the hands on the knees in ghan mudra. Roll up the tongue and shape it as a tube. Inhale maximum air through the tongue. Take the tongue inside the mouth and close the mouth. Then slowly exhale through the nostrils. This is one round of pranayam. Repeat it for four rounds. [Music] Brahri pranayam. Sit in any comfortable posture. Keep the spine and head straight with eyes closed. Place the hands on the knees in gan mudra and inhale deeply through the nose. Exhale slowly making a deep humming sound. [Laughter] Repeat one more round. [Music] Practice the brahri pranayam with shmuki mudra. Using your index fingers, gently close your eyes. Then place your middle finger beside your nose without fully covering it. Place your ring finger on your upper lip and your pinky finger on your lower lip. Finally, rest your thumbs on your ears as demonstrated in the video. Inhale deeply through the nose. Exhale slowly making a deep humming sound. [Laughter] [Music] Repeat one more round. In its resolution, the United Nations General Assembly recognized that yog offers a holistic approach to health and well-being while promoting harmony in all aspects of life. This recognition for wider dissemination of information about the benefits of practicing yog for the health and well-being of world population in India. It ushered in an era of holistic health, a revolution based on the principles that prioritize prevention over cure. The ministry of Aayush successfully organized the first international day of yog on 21st June 2015 at Rajput New Delhi where two Guinness World Records were made. One for the largest yog session involving around 36,000 participants and the other for maximum number of nationalities with participants from 84 countries in a single yog session. Over the years, the celebration of International Day of Yog has gained momentum worldwide with new enthusiasts taking up this ancient form of well-being year by year. In the year 2016, when the second international day of yog was celebrated in Chundigar, another record was created that with largest number of people with disabilities performed together. More than 51,000 yog enthusiasts participated in the third international day of yog celebrations at Lacno in the year 2017. Yog is essentially a spiritual discipline based on an extremely subtle science which focuses on bringing harmony between mind and body. Yog is a practice that combines art with science to provide a guide for a healthy lifestyle. Nowadays all the sections of the society participate and practice yog throughout the year. Yog has now become a mass movement. The international day of yog or IDY celebrations began 100 days before Idy. Many organized across the globe to mark the celebrations as a public event. According to its Sanskrit root yuj, the word yog means to join or to unite and is said to lead to the union of an individual consciousness with the universal consciousness. Jo is widely regarded as an immortal cultural outcome of the Inda Saraswati Valley civilization dating back to 2,700 BC. Although yog was practiced during the prevadic period, it was Maharishi Patanjali who systematized and codified the practices through yog sutras. For his invaluable contribution to this field of knowledge, he is known as the father of classic yo. Later many masters contributed to the preservation and development of yo by documenting the practices and spreading them worldwide. Today, millions of people across the globe are benefiting from the practice of yog. Recent medical research has shown that regular practice of yog offers a variety of physical and mental benefits working on different levels of an individual such as body, energy, mind and emotion. Each system of yog we practice falls within these categories. These have been further adopted and developed by different schools of yog namely Gan Yog, Bakti Yog, Kog, Patanjal Yog, Huti Yog, Kundalini Yog, Dian Yog, Mantra Yog, Lei Yog, Raj Yog, Jane Yog, Bod Yog, etc. Some of the widely practiced yogadan are yam, nam, pranayam, pratyahar, dharana, dyan, sand, mudra, shatarm, yuktahar, mantra, yuktakarma. A yog practitioner should follow the guiding principles of yogic practices before the practices. Yogiic practices should be performed in a calm and quiet atmosphere with relaxed body and mind. It should be done on an empty stomach or light stomach. Consume a small amount of honey in lukewarm water. If you feel weak, light and comfortable cotton clothes are preferred to facilitate easy movements of the body. In case of chronic diseases or pains or cardiac problems, a physician or yog expert should be consulted prior to performing yogic practices. During the practices, practice sessions should start with a prayer or invocation as it creates a conducive environment to relax. Yogic practices should be performed slowly in a relaxed manner with awareness of the body and breath. Do not hold the breath unless it is specially mentioned to do so. Naperville and Chicago and nearby Chicago areas and as you know Yoga is a is an invaluable gift of Indian tradition to the world community we have been working very hard for this celebration today and I've been working very closely with many friends of yoga and yoga enthusiast in Chicago and not only consulate who have been working alone we have a a huge number of volunteers who have come to consulate to assist us to help us in preparation of this great celebration today and I would have to thank each and every organization Friends of yoga who has worked and partner with us in making this event successful as I mentioned yoga is very popular in Chicago and and Chicagoland if I may say so and we have a number of institutions who are practicing yoga promoting yoga yoga or pose provides holistic solution to health and well-being and I am very delighted that so many people from community from our Indian American community has joined hand with the consulate to celebrate 5th international day of yoga in Naperville so I I look forward to the great day today we have number of organizations who have also sort of set up their stalls to tell the community in us as to what they do to promote yoga so all in all I am very excited and very delighted that we have such a huge turnout today to celebrate International Day of yoga in Naperville we have also we will be having lot of dignitaries today who are participating congressman mayor I got a very warm message from the new mayor of Chicago congratulating us on the International Day of yoga so I am as I said earlier Yoga is very popular we have strong support from city governments from the government of Illinois from mayor's office to commemorate this event in a big way in in Chicagoland so thank you very much everybody for participating at the International Day of yoga good morning my name is Steve Jericho I'm the mayor of Naperville today I had the pleasure of having my very first yoga session at the International yoga day here in Naperville Illinois Council general delay delay 'la is up here speaking he was the host and the organizer of this event we're very grateful [Music] [Applause] Thank You congressman mr. idak a smooth easy and mayor mr. Steeves silico and consul general please take your seats now I will request consul general so that allows you to please come to the stage to deliver the welcome address well good morning everybody and welcome to the fifth international day of yoga celebration at this great beautiful town of Naperville Congressman Raja Krishnamurti mayor of Naperville Steve Jericho Friends of yoga all distinguished guests and Yoga lovers who have come in such large number this morning to celebrate with us the fifth international day of yoga in Naperville on behalf of the Indian consulate in Chicago and Friends of yoga I am delighted to welcome everybody this morning to celebrate the International Day of yoga in the beautiful city of neighbor Ville Yoga is an ancient physical mental and spiritual practice that originated in India it is an invaluable gift of ancient Indian traditions to the world community the word yoga is derived from Sanskrit and means to join or to unite symbolizing the union of body and consciousness yoga provides a holistic solution to health and well-being it is in recognition of its growing universal appeal and immense benefits tilde of yoga was introduced by India and it was co-sponsored by a record 177 countries today yoga is practiced in various forms around the word and the International Day of yoga aims to raise awareness worldwide of the several benefits of practicing yoga to my mind Yoga offers a practical approach for the hectic lifestyle that we all follow today few minutes of acog Reza's attention to self and Abbas practice everyday can do wonders to our physical and mental well-being I have recently joined as the Consul General of India in Chicago and I must confess that I have found so many people and organizations who are practicing yoga or trying to sort of raise awareness about yoga in the Chicagoland area and I'm delighted to learn that Yoga is so popular in in Chicago and Midwest region of the u.s. I applaud the services of all yoga masters and yoga gurus and yoga enthusiast like all of you who have gathered this morning in this beautiful facility who have committed themselves to spread the awareness about yoga and its numerous benefits for healthy life several of such organizations and individuals I call them friends of yoga have joined hands with the consulate to organize the international day of yoga this morning and I deeply appreciate their support and enthusiasm for successful conduct of the International Day of yoga today my special thanks to congressman Raja Krishnamurti and Mayor Steve Jericho for joining us this morning and their support for friendship to India and for celebrating the International Day of yoga this year ladies and gentlemen let us give a big round of applause to our very special guest this morning [Applause] friends I truly appreciate your presence here today in celebrating yoga with tactician of masters who are working in spreading the benefit of yoga in communities across the world with these words I invite you to join us in practicing yoga protocol this morning let us try to incorporate the essence of yoga in our daily life jehane thank you sir for the welcome address now I will request congressman mr. Isaac a smooth easy good morning namaste how are you everybody I want to observe those three rules of public speaking be short be sweet and be gone first point can you give a big round of applause to consul general delaila ji and the consulate as well as Mayor Steve chouriço mayor Steve told me that they grew the grass just for this event what a feat thank you so much what a beautiful venue second point give yourselves a big round of applause you brought your families and yourselves and third and final point which is this you know I'm one of four Indian Americans in Congress I affectionately call us the samosa caucus as a proud member of the samosa caucus I'm so happy to say that yoga has expanded in its practice far and wide in the United States just twenty years ago about four million people United States practice yoga today it's 20 million and growing pretty soon it will be 10% of the country isn't that amazing now yoga teaches obviously the practice of meditation but it also teaches the practice of flexibility something we desperately need in Washington DC don't you think so and so I propose that next year we do yoga day on Capitol Hill what do you think I'll close with my favorite saying which is that yesterday is history tomorrow's a mystery but today's a gift that's why we call it the present I'm so honored and blessed to be with you here for just a few short While addressing the 69th session of United Nations General Assembly on September 27th, 2014, the honorable prime minister of India Shri Narendra Modi urged the world community to adopt an international day of yog [Music] which are karma. Fore [Music] mortal sagat. [Music] [Music] [Music] Due to the government of India concerted efforts, 21st June was established. lished as the International Day of Yog in 2014 supported by a record consensus of 177 co-sponsoring countries in 193 member United Nations General Assembly. In its resolution, the United Nations General Assembly recognized that yog offers a holistic approach to health and well-being while promoting harmony in all aspects of life. This recognition highlighted the need for wider dissemination of information about the benefits of practicing yog in India. It ushered in an era of holistic health, a revolution based on the principles that prioritize prevention over cure. The ministry of Aayush successfully organized the first international day of yog on 21st June 2015 at Rajput New Delhi where two Guinness World Records were made. One for the largest yog session involving around 36,000 participants and the other for maximum number of nationalities with participants from 84 countries in a single yog session. Over the years, the celebration of International Day of Yog has gained momentum worldwide with new enthusiasts taking up this ancient form of well-being year by year. In the year 2016 when the second international day of yog was celebrated in Chundigar another record was created that with largest number of people with disabilities perform yog protocol together more than 51,000 yog enthusiasts participated in the third international day of yog celebrations at luckno in the year 2017. Yog is essentially a spiritual discipline based on an extremely subtle science which focuses on bringing harmony between mind and body. Yog is a practice that combines art with science to provide a guide for a healthy lifestyle. Nowadays all the sections of the society participate and practice yog throughout the year. Yog has now become a mass movement. The International Day of Yog or IDY celebrations began 100 days before ID. Many yog programs are being organized across the globe to mark the celebrations as a public event in these 100 days. Various events like yog at heritage sites, iconic places, guardian ring, ocean ring, yogic to antarctic, yog saga mala, yog bharat mala and similar events like these have wider dimensions to ideiy celebrations. The combined efforts and involvement of all stakeholders along with collective approach of the government have made this possible. The practice of common yoke protocol on IDY has become one of the biggest public health practice and initiative which has been adopted by more than 300 million people across the globe. According to its Sanskrit root yuj, the word yog means to join or to unite. And the practice of yog is said to lead to the union of an individual consciousness with the universal consciousness. Yog is widely regarded as an immortal cultural outcome of the Indas Saraswati Valley civilization dating back to 2,700 BC. Although yog was practiced during the prevadic period, it was Maharishi Patanjali who systematized and codified the practices through yog sutras for his invaluable contribution to this field of knowledge. He is known as the father of classic yo. Later many masters contributed to the preservation and development of yo by documenting the practices and spreading them worldwide. Today, millions of people across the globe are benefiting from the practice of yog. Recent medical research has shown that regular practice of yog offers a variety of physical and mental benefits working on different levels of an individual such as body, energy, mind and emotion. Each system of yoke we practice falls within these categories. These have been further adopted and developed by different schools of yog namely Gan Yog, Bhakti Yog, KM Yog, Patanjal Yog, Huti Yog, Kundalini Yog, Dian Yog, Mantra Yog, Lei Yog, Raj Yog, Jane Yog, Bod Yog, etc. Some of the widely practiced yog sadhan are yam, nam, asan, pranayam, pratyahar, dharana, dyan, samadhi, bund, mudra, shatgar, yuktahar, mantra, yuktagarma. A yog practitioner should follow the guiding principles of yogic practices before the practices. Yogiic practices should be performed in a calm and quiet atmosphere with relaxed body and mind. It should be done on an empty stomach or light stomach. Consume a small amount of honey in lukewarm water if you feel weak. Light and comfortable cotton clothes are preferred to facilitate easy movements of the body. In case of chronic diseases or pains or cardiac problems, a physician or yog expert should be consulted prior to performing yogic practices. During the practices, practice sessions should start with a prayer or invocation as it creates a conducive environment to relax the mind. Yogic practices should be performed slowly in a relaxed manner with awareness of the body and breath. Do not hold the breath unless it is specially mentioned to do so. Do not hold the body tightly or jerk the body. Perform the practices according to your own capacity. End the session with deep silence or shanti part. After the practices, food and bath should be taken only 20 to 30 minutes after the practice. Once prepared, step into the world of yog with mindfulness and avail the benefits of this centuries old wisdom. [Music] Let us enter into the world of yog invocation. text: [Music] thank you foreign [Music] thank you for continuing to stay in your spot and be seated we will start the program very shortly and again if you need any assistance whatsoever we have our volunteers standing by to help they're wearing their yellow caps so that you may identify them easily you can see many sculptures that we have here on the North Lawn but today the gandhiji statue is one that is so dear to many of us here the principles that he holds and the concept of ahimsa is one that many of us are very familiar with that many world leaders are embodying and we see this as we look around to many people who aim to be the change that they want to see in the world foreign [Applause] thank you [Applause] excellencies distinguished participants yoga Masters brothers and sisters all of those watching on web TV and joining us today namaste a very warm welcome to everyone happy International Day of yoga 2023. [Applause] this is the time that we've all been waiting for the time has come for me to share how much we're deeply honored we are so privileged to have with us today none other than the honorable prime minister of India SRI Narendra Modi Ji a distinguished world leader and an Ardent yoga practitioner I'd like to remind everyone that it is Prime Minister Modi G himself back in December 2014 who introduced the idea of establishing World yoga day to the UN General Assembly a record number of 175 member states recognized yoga's Universal appeal and adopted The Landmark resolution proclaiming June 21 as International yoga day the day of the summer solstice here in the North so every 21 of June is World yoga day this day provides people everywhere across the globe all of us and everyone watching an opportunity to reflect upon the countless benefits of practicing yoga indeed an invaluable gifts in the world from India's ancient tradition we thank the honorable prime minister of India for this notable initiative which is now celebrated worldwide with enthusiasm and vigor I will now begin the official part of today's event by requesting Ambassador ruchira camboge India's permanent representative to the United Nations to the stage to deliver her opening remarks [Applause] honorable prime minister SRI Narendra Modi ji Mr President of the general assembly Madam deputy secretary General Mr Mayor distinguished ambassadors distinguished guests and all my dear friends and colleagues from this wonderful audience today's celebration is indeed very very special prime minister Narendra Modi will lead us in doing yoga here at the United Nations lawns to put matters in perspective it was under his leadership and under his guidance that 21st June was declared as International yoga day and it is only fitting therefore that the inspiration behind the idea is with us today in this Scenic Multicultural setting of The United Nations ladies and gentlemen it is truly beautiful to see so many around the world Gather in solidarity for yoga it is not living with the burden of wheat or the lightness of Oblivion it is a choice made with balance an idiosyncratic Beauty Timeless throughout the progress of humanity yoga has endured it is truth Beyond articulation wisdom Beyond Time I hope all of you will cherish 21st June this year and hold it close to your hearts today and forever thank you [Applause] thank you Ambassador camboge for your remarks it's my turn now to invite The Honorable president of the U.N General Assembly Mr Chaba Karoshi for the stage [Applause] honorable prime minister Madame deputy secretary General Mr Mayor Madam ambassador dear friends namaste yoga is a way of uniting mind and body and also the environment and us the most memorable University lectures I attended as a student many years ago where on cultural traditions of ancient Eastern civilizations they helped me understand that we are part of nature despite regarding ourselves as aristocratically detached from other creatures I also learned that the energies in the universe and inside us abide by the same law a desire to achieve balance yoga changes our physical performance but more importantly it can spark in US different mental and intellectual performance with a control that it teaches over ourselves we learn how to make change and impact since that University lecture have been an admirer of yoga I'm very proud of my daughter who has already reached a high level of practice and is continuing her journey thanks to her I understand better yoga's ethical guide guide the guide which includes the principle of non-stealing non-greed non-harding and non-violence in other words yoga's ethical guide To Living is a guide to sustainability not overusing resources that belong to all of us not prioritizing short-term gains over planetary boundaries and working for transformation our world needs balance self-control and creativity just as it needs recognition of moral responsibilities and cherishing peace and justice there are many ways to approach these aims yoga is one of the means for which I have a special respect I'm happy to be here with all of you today but let me apologize in advance I am not a yoga protection practitioner and the positions which you will show might look very different from what I end up doing still I'm here with an open heart and open mind thank you very much [Applause] thank you president Karoshi for highlighting the transformative power of yoga and in such a very personal way at this time I would now ask that mayor Eric Adams the mayor of the city of New York please take the stage [Applause] to Prime Minister Modi to president of the general assembly to deputy secretary General to your excellencies to the Ambassador to all of you do not miss the significance of this moment New York City is the largest and most important city in America as I stand before you as the mayor of that City I share with you in a clear and a universal tone that we are no longer focused solely on the physical body but the anatomy of our spirit for far too long we have separated what our needs are emotionally spiritually and physically we are going to go to the root causes of that which troubles many of us from how we interact to the food we eat to allowing our children to have breathing exercises in the morning as they start this school day to challenge the mental health issue that's impacting us but there's a larger challenge for all of us what we do on the mat and practicing yoga must become what we do on the streets and in our countries we cannot become universally connected through the yellow match we lay on but disconnected as we move through our communities use the spirit and emotions of yoga to challenge and fight against War to fight against gender oppression to fight against violence to fight against a lack of clean water to fight against the food insecurity to fight against the destruction of our planet our connectivity is not only how we're connected to our families and our parents we have two mothers one gave birth to us and one sustains us yoga keeps us connected to both mothers and connected to each other this International yoga day is an international call for us to stand up for humankind across the globe and all living being I'm proud to be here and I'm proud to symbolically represent this city as stating the international yoga day is an international call that we will be a leader here in New York City as you are a leader in your own environments thank you for allowing me to Rejoice with you today thank you mayor Adams for spotlighting how yoga is integral to the anatomy of one's Spirit we appreciate that perspective and input I'd like now to go to the video message of the UN Secretary General Antonio Gutierrez who is not available to be with us in person but is here in spirit please let us go to the videotape happy International Day of yoga I want to recognize Prime Minister Modi for all he has done to promote understanding of yoga and its many benefits yoga unites it unites body and mind humanity and nature and millions of people across the globe for whom it is a source of strength Harmony and peace in a dangerous and divided world the benefits of this ancient practice are particularly precious yoga offers a Haven of calm it can reduce anxiety and promote mental well-being it helps us to develop discipline and patience it connects us to our planet which so badly needs our protection and it reveals our text: Yoga helps us find that balance one breath at a time. It heals, strengthens, and grounds us in the present moment. Good morning, ladies and gentlemen. I welcome you to the 10th anniversary of the International Day of Yoga. I extend a very warm welcome to the distinguished heads of missions and thank you for joining us this morning. Excellencies, we are honored to have you amongst us. And we have in our midst now the honorable External Affairs Minister of India, Dr. S. Jaishankar. We extend a very hearty welcome to the honorable Minister on this occasion. Ladies and gentlemen, excellencies, distinguished guests, we are at the 10th anniversary of the International Day of Yoga, and this celebration is organized by the Ministry of External Affairs here at the historic G20 tree plantation area in Nehru Park, New Delhi. Yoga, a Sanskrit word which means to unite or to connect, is more than just a practice. It is India's gift to global well-being, symbolizing the harmony of body, mind, and spirit. And today, as we mark a decade of this universal celebration, we do so with friends and diplomatic missions from across the world. This year, we celebrate under the theme Yoga for one earth, one health, a powerful call to care not only for ourselves, but for each other and the planet we share. From nurturing individual well-being to inspiring systemic change, India's evolving yoga landscape is shaping a healthier, more harmonious world. Ladies and gentlemen, we are also honored to have amidst us three distinguished yoga practitioners from Argentina, Singapore, and Kazakhstan. They are here as part of a 17-member delegation from 15 countries to participate in the Yog Bandhan initiative to celebrate the 10th anniversary of the International Day of Yoga. It is inspiring that the number of yoga events that are being held worldwide has doubled this year from last year. And this time there are more than 2,000 yoga events held in over 1,300 locations in 191 countries. 191 countries, ladies and gentlemen, of the world. So, every country in the world is celebrating the International Day of Yoga with us. And now, we begin with a common yoga protocol session, 45 minutes of mindfulness, movement, and inner harmony. May this morning energize your day and deepen our collective commitment to health and balance. I now invite our yoga instructor to please lead us. Thank you, ma'am. Namaste. Very good morning to all of you. And a very happy International Day of Yoga. I welcome you all in the common yoga protocol session. So, without any further delay, let us begin the session. I request everyone to please sit down at your place. Sit in any comfortable posture known as Sukhasana with the back and neck straight. We begin the session with prayer. Join your palms in Namaskar Mudra. Bring your hands close to the chest. Gently close your eyes. May you move in harmony. May you speak in unison. Let our mind be equanimous like in the beginning. Let the divinity manifest in all our endeavors. With this power we'll chant the prayer. Gently rub your palms. Make cup of your hands. Keep it on your eyes. Gently remove and open your eyes. Now we are moving toward the loosening practices. The loosening practices help to increase microcirculation. And these practices we will do in standing position. So I request everyone to please stand up at your place. Stand with the feet 2 to 3 in just apart. Keep the hands straight beside the body. Samasthiti. Now keep your hands on the waist with the fingers in front, thumb at the back. Now inhale and slowly take your neck back. Exhale and slowly bend forward. Try to touch the chin to the chest. Once again, we'll repeat this practice. Inhale, take your neck back. Exhale and take your neck back. Exhale, bend forward. Inhale, come back to the starting position. Now, we'll bend our neck to the right side. Exhale and slowly bend your neck toward the right side. Try to bring your ear close to the shoulder joint. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale, bend toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. Repeat this practice two more times. Exhale, bend your neck toward the right side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale, toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. Now, we are turning the neck toward the side. Exhale and slowly move your neck toward the right side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. Repeat this practice two more times. Exhale, turn your neck toward the right side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale, turn your neck toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale toward the right side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. Bring your arms to the side. Close your eyes and feel the impact of the practice. Open your eyes. Now we are moving toward the next practice. Shoulder movement. Inhale, lift your arms from the sides with the palms facing outside. Exhale and slowly bring your arms down. Inhale, lift your arms up. Exhale. Bring your arms down. Inhale. Lift your arms up. Exhale. Bring your arms down. Now, we are doing the shoulder rotation. Inhale. Lift your arms up to the level of shoulder. Turn your wrist upward. Bend your elbow and place the fingers on the respective shoulder joint. Bring the elbows in in front. Inhale. And take your elbows up. Take them back. Exhale. Bring them down, then in front. Bring the elbows in front. We'll repeat this practice two more times. Inhale up. Exhale down. Join the elbows in front. Inhale up. Exhale down. Join the elbows in front. We'll repeat this practice in the opposite direction. Exhale down. Inhale up. Join the elbows in front. Repeat this practice two more times. Exhale down. Inhale up. Exhale down. Inhale up. Now, straighten your arms from the sides. Exhale, bring your arms down. Close your eyes and feel the impact of the practice. Now, moving to the next practice movement. Stand up with the feet shoulder width apart. Inhale and slowly lift your arms up to the level of shoulder with the palms facing each other. Exhale and twist your spine toward the right side keeping the right arm straight, left elbow bent. Look at the thumb of the right hand. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. We'll repeat this practice two more times. Exhale toward the right side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale toward the right side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale toward the left side. Inhale, come back to the center. Exhale, bring your arms down to the side, close your eyes, and feel the impact of the practice. Open your eyes. The next practice is knee movement. If someone is suffering from osteoarthritis of the knee joint, they should avoid this practice. Inhale, lift your arms up to the level of shoulder with the palms facing down. Exhale, and slowly bend down. Come in the semi-squat position. Imagine there is a chair behind you, and sit on that imaginary chair. Inhale, and slowly come up. Repeat this practice two more times. Exhale, and slowly bend down. Inhale, come up. Exhale, and slowly bend down. Keeping the back straight. Inhale, and slowly come up. Bring your arms down. Close your eyes, and feel the impact of the practice. Now, open your eyes. We are moving toward the asana practice. Join your feet together. Or you can stand with the feet 2 in apart. Now, inhale, and slowly lift your arms to the level of shoulder with the palms facing each other. Interlace your fingers. Turn your wrist outward. Inhale, slowly lift your arms up with the elbows attached to the ears. Stretch your spine. Now, slowly lift your heels up, and stand up on your toes. Maintain the posture with normal breathing. Tadasana stretches your spine, improves flexibility of the calf muscles. Exhale, slowly bring your heels down, and the arms down from the side. The next practice is Ardha Chakrasana. The word Ardha means half. Chakra means wheel. The body assumes the position of half wheel in this posture. If someone is suffering from vertigo or severe back issues, hypertension, they can avoid this practice. This practice is very beneficial for strengthening the back muscles and improving the lungs capacity. Stand straight with the feet shoulder width apart to make it comfortable. Place your hands at the side of the waist with the fingers in front, thumb at the back. Take your elbows and shoulders back. Slightly pull them back. Now, inhale and slowly bend backward. Take the neck back, chest back. Bend from the lower back, the lumbosacral region. Make Exhale and slowly come back. Bring your arms down and feel the impact of the practice. You can also close your eyes. Open your eyes. The next practice we are doing Padahastasana. For this, I request everyone to please join your legs together. Bring your arms in front at the front of your thighs. Inhale, lift your arms up, stretch up. Stretch up your spine. If someone is suffering from hypertension, severe back issue, please avoid the practice. Exhale and slowly bend down. Slowly keeping the back straight, try to bring your hands at the side of the feet or you can catch your ankle and try to bring your head close to Maintain the posture with normal breathing. Padahastasana is very good for making the spine flexible, improves digestion. Inhale and slowly come up by stretching your spine upward. Exhale, bring your arms down from the side. Now we are moving toward the sitting posture. I request everyone to sit down in the long sitting position. With the legs straight in front. Hands at the side of the buttocks with the fingers pointing forward. Keeping the back straight, this is a starting position. Known Dandasana. We are moving toward Bhadrasana. For Bhadrasana, if someone is suffering from knee problem, they should avoid this practice, like osteoarthritis of the knees or any knee injuries. The word Bhadra means auspicious. This practice is very good for strengthening the pelvic region and very good for females, specially. Bend both the knees. Join the soles of the feet together. Interlace your fingers. Catch your toes. Bring your heels close to the perineum region. Keeping the back straight, close your eyes. Maintain the posture with normal breathing. You can close your eyes. Exhale and slowly come back. Release your hands, straighten your legs. Relax. Now we are moving toward the next practice, Vajrasana. This is considered as one of the meditative posture. This practice is very good for improving your digestion. If anyone is suffering from knee problem or ankle injuries, they should avoid this practice or do with with caution. Now, take your hands to the side. Bend both the knees. Join your big toes, and the heels apart. Keep the knees together. Place your hands on the thighs. Close your eyes. Maintain the posture with normal breathing. This practice also strengthens the thigh and calf muscles. Relax. Now we are moving toward the next practice. Please open your eyes. From the same position, I request everyone to stand up on your knees, come in the kneeling position. We'll be doing ardha ushtrasana, the half camel pose. In the final version of this asana, the body resembles the hump of a camel. This practice is very beneficial for constipation, for strengthening the back muscles, and improving the lungs capacity. Now, place the hands on the hips with the fingers pointing downward. Keeping the elbows and shoulder parallel, taking the elbows and shoulders back, inhale and slowly bend backward. Keeping the thighs perpendicular to the ground. Maintain the posture with normal breathing. Exhale and slowly come back and sit in vajrasana again. Close your eyes and feel the impact of the practices. Next, we'll be doing the counter pose, shashakasana. The word shashak means moon. And also rabbit. the whole body and become aware of all the sounds around you any sounds that you hear notice them and let the sounds be there as they are you are in harmony with the environment as it is take a deep breath in and breathe out become aware of any thoughts that are floating through the mind do not resist any thoughts let them come now you are in harmony with the thoughts with the mind and become aware of any feelings Pleasant or unpleasant accept the feelings as they are you are peace you are Joy breathe in and let go again become aware of your feelings become aware of your thoughts become aware of your whole body and let's immerse ourselves in the sounds [Music] foreign [Music] foreign [Music] [Music] [Music] foreign [Music] [Music] take a deep breath in and breathe out a long deep breath in and breathe out become aware of your whole body and where you're Seated on the Earth breathe in and breathe out and slowly and gently when you feel ready you can gradually open your eyes good namaste Om Shanti Shanti Om everyone what an uplifting and revitalizing yoga session we've had and the sun even started to come out even more shining its Rays upon us so gloriously we are here in this beautiful North Lawn of the United Nations Headquarters in New York City celebrating World yoga day 2023 a sincere thank you annalize Richmond and to all our Young Yoga practitioners who've helped to demonstrate during this yoga session we have an unforgettable event with them and for the youngsters in the first row leading us as well this morning one more round of thanks to them as they exit the stage and take their leave namaste namaste have a beautiful day our deepest gratitude to The Honorable prime minister of India Narendra modiji for gracing us with his presence and in creating this wonderful memory at the iconic United United Nations Headquarters that we cherish from years to come friends I am going to ask if you can please stay in your seats please remain seated while The Honorable prime minister of India meets and greets participants The Honorable prime minister will meet and greet participants may I ask for your cooperation and your understanding friends may I ask that you remain seated at your yoga mats please as the dignitaries and his Excellency Prime Minister Modi ji meets and greets and mingles with some people excellencies and friends I have some incredible news to share a Guinness World Record is created today today we have gone for a Guinness World Record now in the books it's a world record for the most nationalities in a yoga lesson the most nationalities in a yoga lesson right here at New York un headquarters big congratulations to all of us for being part of this historic event to share more details and can and conducting this formal proceeding I'd like to have the Guinness World Records official adjudicator Mr Michael emprick to the stage I'm going to ask people to please please remain at your mats please allow his Excellency what foreign foreign foreign foreign foreign foreign foreign foreign [Applause]

Dunya kiss of Santa man home yoga dwara a home yoga done they suck that my up with the mental garden home Philip antarastriya yoga divas the name making yoga John Cena the garden Kelly 84 that participated in a single yoga session. Millions of people participated with full enthusiasm in this first International Day of Yoga celebrations across the globe. Yoga is essentially a spiritual discipline based on extremely subtle science. It is an art and science for healthy living. The word yoga is derived from the Sanskrit word yoga meaning to join or unite. According to yogic scriptures, the practice of yoga leads to the union of the individual consciousness with that of the universal consciousness. Indicating a perfect balance between mind and body, man and nature. The science of yoga has its origins thousands of years ago before the first religions or belief systems were born. In yogic lore, Shiva, the first yogi or Adi Yogi, transmitted his profound knowledge to his seven disciples, the famous Saptarishis or the seven sages. Yoga was practiced through the pre-Vedic period. But, it was Maharishi Patanjali, known as the father of modern yoga, who systematized and codified the then practices through his Yoga Sutras. Later, many yoga masters contributed to the preservation and development of yoga and the spreading of this wonderful practice. There are different schools of yoga. These include Jnana yoga, Bhakti yoga, Karma yoga, Patanjala yoga, Hatha yoga, Kundalini yoga, Dhyana yoga, Mantra yoga, Laya yoga, Jaina yoga, Bauddha yoga, etc. Each school has its own approach and practices that lead to the ultimate aims and objectives of yoga. Some of the widely practiced yoga sadhanas are Yama, Niyama, Asana, Pranayama, Pratyahara, Dharana, Dhyana, Samadhi, Bandha, and Mudra, Shatkarmas, Yukta Ahara, Mantra Japa, Yukta Karma. A yoga practitioner should follow the guiding principles while performing yogic practices. Before practice, yogic practice should be performed in a calm and quiet atmosphere with a relaxed body and mind. It should be done on an empty stomach or light stomach. Consume a small amount of honey in lukewarm water if you feel weak. Light and comfortable cotton clothes are preferred to facilitate easy movement of the body. In case of chronic diseases or pain or cardiac a physician or a yoga therapist should be consulted prior to performing yogic practices. During practice, practice sessions should start with a prayer or invocation as it creates a conducive environment to relax the mind. Yogic practices shall be performed slowly in a relaxed manner with awareness of the body and breath. Do not hold the breath unless it is specially mentioned to do so. Do not hold the body tightly or jerk the body. Perform the practices according to your own capacity. End the session with deep silence or Shanti path. Food or a bath may be taken only 20 to 30 minutes after practice. Welcome to the world of yoga. Welcome to the world of wisdom that is as relevant and beneficial today as it was thousands of years ago. Sam gachhadhwam sam vadadhwam Welcome to the world of yoga. Let us start yoga sadhana with chalana kriyas or loosening practices to increase microcirculation. Neck exercises, forward and backward neck bending. Stand in an alert posture. Keep feet comfortably apart and your arms on the waist. Exhale and bend head forward slowly and try to touch the chin to the chest. Inhale. Move the head as far back as is comfortable and come back with inhalation. Right and left neck bending. Exhale, bend the head slowly to the right. Bring the ear as close as possible to the shoulder. Inhale, bring the head to normal position. Exhale, bend the head to the left side. Inhale, bring the head up to normal position. Right and left twisting. Exhale, gently turn the head to the right side so that the chin is in line with the shoulder. Inhale, bring the head to the normal position. Exhale, turn the head to the left side. Inhale, and bring the head to the normal position. Neck rotation. Bend the head forward trying to touch the chin to the chest. Inhale, slowly rotate the head clockwise. While coming down, exhale. Rotate the head in anti-clockwise direction. Feel the stretch around the neck and loosening up of the joints and muscles of the neck and release of tension in the neck. People with neck pain and spondylitis should do the practice gently. Shoulder movements. Feet together, make the body straight. The arms by the sides. Inhale, raise both the arms sideways above your head with the palms outwards. Exhale, and bring them down in the same manner. The arms must not touch the head when going up or the thighs when coming down. The palms must be open with the fingers together. Shoulder rotation. Stand erect. Raise both the arms. Place the fingers of the left hand on the left shoulder and the fingers of the right hand on the right shoulder. Full rotation of both the elbows in a circular manner. Try to touch the elbows in front of the chest on the forward movement and touch the ears while moving up. Stretch the arm back in the backward movement and touch the sides of the trunk while coming down. Do the same anticlockwise. And repeat five times. Practice of this kriya makes the bones, muscles, and nerves of the shoulder region healthy. These practices are helpful in cervical spondylitis and frozen shoulder. Trunk twisting. Keep the legs about 2 ft apart. Raise both the arms up to chest level with the palms facing each other. Exhale. Twist the body towards the right side so that the left palm touches the right shoulder. Come back with inhalation. Now, exhale. Twist the body towards the left side so that the right palm touches the left shoulder. Come back with inhalation. Repeat. Do slowly with breathing. Avoid this practice in case of vertebral and disc disorders and during menstruation. Relax in standing posture. Knee movements. Inhale. Lift your arms up at the shoulder level. Exhale. Bend the knees and bring down your body to the squatting position. Inhale and straighten the body. Repeat two more times. This practice strengthens the knees and hip joints. Avoid this in case suffering from acute arthritis. Exhale while bringing down the hands. Now, let us move to yoga asana. Tadasana. Tad means palm tree. Keep your feet 2 in apart. Interlock the fingers and turn the wrist outwards. Inhale. Raise the arms up and bring them in line with the shoulders. Raise the heels off the floor and balance on the toes. Stay in this position for 10 to 15 seconds. This asana brings stability in the body. Helps to clear up congestion of the spinal nerves. Corrects faulty posture. Exhale. Bring the heels down. Release the interlock of the fingers and bring the arms down parallel to the trunk and come back to a standing posture. Vrikshasana Vriksh means tree. Stand with the feet 2 in apart. Focus on a point in front. Exhale, bend the right leg and place the foot on the inside of the left thigh. The heel should be touching the perineum. Inhale and extend the arms up and join the palms. Stay in this position for 10 to 15 seconds and breathe normally. This improves neuromuscular coordination, endurance, and alertness. Avoid this practice in case of arthritis and obesity. Exhale and bring the arms and right foot down. And repeat the asana. Bend the left leg and place the foot on the right thigh. The heel should touch the perineum. Inhale. Extend the arms up and join the palms. Relax. Padahastasana Pada means foot. Hasta means hand. Inhale slowly and raise the arms up. Stretch up the body from the waist. Exhale and bend forward until the entire palm rests on the ground. Maintain this final posture for 10 to 30 seconds. Those who have a stiff back should bend according to their capacity. Now, inhale. Come up slowly to the vertical position and stretch the arms above the head. Exhale and slowly return to the starting position in reverse order. Relax. Ardha Chakrasana. Support the back at the waist. Exhale. Drop the head backwards, stretch the neck muscles, and bend backwards from the lumbar region. Relax with normal breathing. Stay there for 10 to 30 seconds. Ardha Chakra makes the spine flexible and strengthens the spinal nerves and improves breathing capacity. Inhale and slowly come up and relax. Hypertensive patients shall bend with care. Trikonasana. Trikon means triangle. Stand with your feet comfortably apart. Slowly raise both the arms sideways till they are horizontal. Exhale. Slowly bend to the right side and place the right hand just behind the right foot. The left arm is straight up in line with the right arm. Remain in this posture for 10 to 30 seconds with normal breathing. This prevents flat foot, strengthens the calf, thigh, and waist muscles, makes the spine flexible. As you inhale, slowly come up. Repeat for the left side. Avoid this posture in case of slipped disc, sciatica, and after undergoing abdominal surgery. Do not go beyond your limits. Bhadrasana. Let us now get ready for sitting postures. Bhadrasana. Bhadra means firm. Sit erect with the legs stretched out straight in front. Keep the hands beside the hips. This is dandasana. Now put the soles of your feet together. Exhale and clasp your hands together over your toes. Pull your heels as close as possible up to the perineum region. This is the final position. Stay here for some time. Bhadrasana keeps the body firm and stabilizes the mind. Now stretch your leg and come to vishramasana. Avoid this practice in case of severe arthritis and sciatica. Vajrasana. Sit in dandasana. Fold your legs and sit on your heels. Keep the thighs closed and big toes touching. Place the hands on the knees. The head and back should be straight. This is Vajrasana. Ardha Ushtrasana. Sit in Vajrasana. Get up on your knees. Place the hands on the waist with the fingers pointing downwards. Keep the elbows and shoulders parallel. Bend the head back and stretch the neck muscles. And bend the trunk backwards as much as possible. As you exhale, relax. Remain in this posture for 10 to 30 seconds with normal breathing. It helps to strengthen the back and neck muscles, relieves constipation and back pain. Return with inhalation. Sit in Vajrasana. Relax. Now we perform the full version of Ushtrasana. Sit in Vajrasana. Kneel down on the floor. Keep your thighs and feet together. Get up on your knees. Bring the knees and the feet a few inches apart. While inhaling, bend backwards. Place the right palm on the right heel and the left palm on the left heel. Be careful not to jerk the neck while bending backwards. Ushtrasana is extremely useful for defective eyesight. This is useful in relieving back pain and neck pain. It helps to reduce fat over the abdomen and hips. Those suffering from high blood pressure, heart disease, and hernia should not practice this. Return with inhalation. Sit in Vajrasana. Relax. Shashankasana. Shashank means head. Spread both the knees wide apart. Keep the toes touching. Keep the palms between the knees. Exhale and slowly stretch them full length. Bend forward and place the chin on the ground. Keep the arms parallel. Look in front and maintain the posture. This helps to reduce stress and anger. Inhale and come up. Exhale and come back to Vajrasana. Stretch your legs back to Vishramasana. Patients with osteoarthritis should do this with caution. Uttan Mandukasana. The final position of Uttan Mandukasana resembles an upright frog, hence the name. Sit in Vajrasana. Spread both the knees wide apart while the toes remain together. Inhale. Raise both the arms. Then cross both arms behind the head and place the hands on the upper part of the opposite shoulders. Keep the back and neck straight. Maintain this position for a while. This asana is helpful in back ache and cervical pain. It helps in improving diaphragmatic movement and helps to improve lung capacity. Persons with severe knee joint pain should not perform it. While coming back, slowly raise both the arms with inhalation. Bring back and bring the knees together as in the initial position. Vakrasana. Vakra means twisted. Bend the right leg and place the right foot beside the left knee. Bring the left arm around the right knee and place the palm beside the right foot. Exhale. Twist the body and neck to the right. Comfortably remain in this posture. Turn your head back. Take out your hands with exhalation. Stretch your legs. Now, come back and relax in Vishramasana. This asana increases flexibility of the spine. It helps to overcome constipation, dyspepsia, and in the management of diabetes. Repeat the same on the other side. Turn your head back. Take out your hands with exhalation. Stretch your legs. Now, come back and relax in Vishramasana. Makarasana Makara means crocodile. Lie down on your stomach with the feet wide apart. Feet pointing outward. Rest your head on your hands. Relax. This is Makarasana. This asana is practiced for relaxation in all prone postures. It promotes relaxation of the lower back. Bhujangasana Now, let us be ready for prone postures. Bhujangasana Bhujang means snake or cobra. Lie down on your stomach. Rest your head on your hands and relax the body. Now, join your legs and stretch your arms. Keep the forehead on the ground. Keep your palms beside the chest and raise the elbows. Inhale, lift the chin and chest up to the navel region. This is Bhujangasana. This asana is best for stress management. It also helps to manage back ache and bronchial problems. Exhale. Rest your forehead on the ground and stretch your arms. Spread your legs. Place your palms and rest your head on the palms and relax. Salabhasana. Salabha means a locust. Lie down on your stomach. Now, join your legs. Rest the chin on the floor. Keep both hands beside the body. Palms facing upwards. Inhale. Raise the legs off the floor as much as you can without bending the knees. Extend the arms and legs well to ease the lift of the body off the floor. Stay in position comfortably. This asana helps in sciatica and lower back ache. It tones the hip muscles. Now, exhale. Bring the legs down towards the floor. Take out your arms and rest on the floor. Cardiac patients should avoid this posture. Setu Bandhasana. Now, we move to supine postures. Setu Bandhasana. Setu Bandh means formation of a bridge. Join your legs. Keep the arms beside the body. Bend both the legs at the knees and bring the heels near the buttocks. Hold both the ankles firmly. Inhale. Slowly raise your buttocks and trunk up as much as you can to form a bridge. Remain comfortably. This is the final position. This asana relieves depression and anxiety. It strengthens the lower back muscles. Now, exhale. Slowly return to the original position and relax. Lie down on your back. Please note, women in advanced stages of pregnancy should not practice this asana. Uttanpadasana, raised feet posture. In this asana, the legs are raised upward in a supine position, hence the name. Lie comfortably on the ground with the legs stretched out. The hands should be placed by the sides. While inhaling, slowly raise both the legs without bending them at the knees and bring them to a 30° angle. Maintain this position with normal breathing. Exhale and slowly bring both the legs down and place them on the ground. It balances the navel center, nabhi manipura chakra. Ardha Halasana, half plow posture. This posture is known as ardha Halasana because in its final position, the body resembles half the shape of an Indian plow. Take a supine position. Keep the hands by the sides of the thighs, palms resting on the ground. Slowly raise your legs together without bending at the knees. First raise the legs up to 30°, then further up to a 60° angle. Now, slowly raise the legs to a 90° angle. This is the final position of the Ardha Halasana. The body from the hip to the shoulder should be kept straight. Then slowly bring the legs back to the ground without lifting the head. This is very beneficial for hypertensive patients, but needs to be practiced with care. Those who have lumbosacral or lower back pain should not perform this with both legs together. Pawanmuktasan Bend both the knees and bring the thighs to the chest. Interlock the fingers and clasp the shin below the knee. Raise the head and shoulders. Try to touch the knees with the chin. This is Pawanmuktasana. This removes constipation. Gives relief from flatulence and tones up the back muscles and spinal nerves. Bring your head back. Exhale. Stretch your legs and relax. Avoid this practice in case of abdominal injuries, hernia, sciatica, and during pregnancy. Shavasana. Shav means the dead body. This asana is meant for complete relaxation. Lie down on your back with the arms and legs comfortably apart. Palms facing upward. Eyes closed. Relax the whole body consciously. Become aware of natural breath. And allow it to become rhythmic and slow. Remain in this position till you feel refreshed and relaxed. This asana helps relieve all kinds of tensions and gives rest to both the body and the mind. It is very beneficial in the management of stress and its consequences. Kapalabhati. It and stimulates respiratory centers in the frontal brain. Sit in any meditative posture. Close the eyes and relax the whole body. Inhale deeply. Expand the chest. Expel the breath with forceful contractions of the abdominal muscles. Continue active exhalation and passive inhalation. Then take a deep breath. Exhale slowly and relax. This is one round of Kapalabhati. Each round should be followed by deep breathing. Now, do two more rounds. Kapalabhati purifies the frontal air sinuses. Helps to overcome cough disorders. It is useful in treating colds, rhinitis, sinusitis, asthma, and bronchial infections. Please avoid this practice in case of cardiac conditions, high blood pressure, vertigo, migraine, stroke, hernia, and gastric ulcers. Pranayama, Nadi Shodhana or Anulom Vilom Pranayama is alternate breathing through the left and right nostrils. Sit in a meditative posture. Keep the spine straight and the head erect with eyes closed. Relax the body with a few deep breaths. Keep the left palm on the left knee in Gyan Mudra. The right hand should be in the Nasikagra Mudra. Place the right thumb on the right nostril. Breathe in from the left nostril. Close the left nostril. Exhale through the right nostril. Next, inhale through the right nostril. Close the right nostril and exhale through the left nostril. This is one round of Nadi Shodhana or Anulom Vilom Pranayama. Repeat for another five rounds. For beginners, the duration of inhalation and exhalation should be equal. Gradually, make it 1:2 inhalation, exhalation. The breath should be slow, steady, and controlled. The main purpose of this pranayama is to purify the principal channels of energy. This nourishes the whole body. It induces tranquility. Helps to improve concentration. It increases vitality. And lowers the level of stress and anxiety. It also elevates cough disorders. Sheetali pranayama. Sheet means cool. It also means calm and passionless. As the name indicates, this pranayama cools the mind-body system. Sit in the padmasana or any other comfortable sitting posture. Place the hands on the knees in the Gyan mudra or Anjali mudra. Roll the tongue in from the sides to shape it as a tube. Inhale through this tube-shaped tongue. Fill the lungs with air to their maximum capacity and close the mouth. And then slowly exhale through the nostrils. Sheetali pranayama purifies the blood. It has a cooling effect on the body. It is beneficial for persons suffering from high blood pressure. It satisfies thirst and appeases hunger. It relieves indigestion and disorders caused by phlegm, cough, and bile. It is beneficial for the skin and the eyes. Those who are suffering from cold, cough, or tonsillitis should not do this pranayama. Brahmari pranayama Brahmarah means a black bee. During the exhalation of this pranayama, the sound produced resembles the buzzing of a black bee. Inhale deeply through both nostrils. Exhale slowly in a controlled manner making a deep, steady humming sound as that of a black bee. This is one round of brahmari. Repeat two more times. This is a great tranquilizer. It's found good in the management of stress-related disorder. Now, do the brahmari with shanmukhi mudra. Close your ears with your thumbs. Place the forefingers on your eyes, middle fingers on the nostrils, ring finger and small fingers on the lips. Open your nose and inhale through both nostrils. Exhale slowly in a controlled manner, making the deep humming sound of a black bee. It is a useful preparatory pranayama for concentration and meditation. Now, let us prepare for dhyan. Dhyan, or meditation, is an act of continuous contemplation. Sit in any meditative posture. Keep your spine comfortably erect. Hold jnana mudra. Keep your palms facing upwards upon the thighs. Arms and shoulders should be loose and relaxed. Close your eyes and sit with a slightly upturned face. You need not concentrate. Just maintain a mild focus between the eyebrows and be conscious of your breath. Dhyan keeps the mind calm and quiet. It increases concentration, memory, clarity of thought, and will It rejuvenates the whole body and mind, giving them proper rest. It helps to develop positive emotions. Meditation leads to self-realization. Continue to be in this state of dhyan. Ooh. Ooh. Ooh. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Mhm. Let us now take some cup. I commit to make myself into a healthy, peaceful, joyful, and loving human being. Through every action of mine, I will strive to create a peaceful and loving atmosphere around me. I strive to break the limitations of who I am right now and include the entire world as my own. I recognize the kinship of my own life with every other life. I recognize the unity of all that is. End the yoga practice session with Shanti path or universal prayer. Om Shanti Shanti Shanti Mhm.

The first day marking the auspicious beginning of our 21-day yoga for unity challenge. This is a very special journey that will lead us together towards the celebration of the international day of yoga for the year 2026. It is an honor, a joy, a privilege to be together on the first day of this preparatory phase for the challenge. Heartfulness is delighted to also welcome all friends, family members, brothers and sisters who are joining us to practice with us live both from the meditation hall at Kanha Shanti Vanam and those who are joining us online. Over the next 21 days, thousands of people from more than 160 countries, cultures, and backgrounds are coming together each day in a shared spirit of harmony and well-being. Through the practice of asana, pranayyama and heartfulness meditation, we will not only be nurturing our physical and mental health, but we will also experience the deeper unity that yoga inspires among all of us. Yoga reminds us that beyond our differences, we are all connected by a common humanity. As we breathe together, move together and meditate together, we create a powerful field of collective peace and unity that extends far beyond these sessions. We are especially happy to share that from June 15th onwards, Heartfulness Global Yoga Teachers will be offering daily classes at convenient morning timings locally across the world in more than 10 languages. Mandarin, Korean, Vietnamese, English, Arabic, Farsy, French, Italian, German, Spanish and Portuguese. This way throughout the day from one time zone to another people across the world will be coming together in practice creating a living wave of yoga for unity that circles the earth and we are all going to be a part of it. We invite all friends, family, brothers and sisters to join us throughout this entire 21-day journey. Also invite those we know, those we care for to register for the challenge if you have not already done so. All registered participants will continue to rem to receive updates, resources and an official certificate of participation upon completion of the program. It's done. Bring attention to your abdomen area. Relax your abdomen area. Bring attention to your chest. Relax your chest area. Bring attention to your shoulders. Let all the tensions melt away from your shoulders. Bring attention to your upper arms, lower arms, all the way up to your fingertips. Your arms completely relaxed. Bring attention to your neck. Relax your neck. Bring attention to your face. Relax all your facial muscles from top of your head to the toes. The entire body completely relaxed. Now slowly bring your attention back to your body. You can wiggle your toes, your fingers. Now slowly turn into any one side. Keep your eyes closed. Now with the help of your arms, come to a seated posture. Bring attention to your heart. Sit comfortably and observe yourself.

text: [Music] hello I'm Laura lemon tree and today I'm going to take you through a yoga flow that you can do sitting and lying down on the floor please be careful when you get down onto the floor and up again after the class you don't need a yoga mat just make sure you're feeling comfortable you'll need enough space to lie down with your arms stretched out or by the sides I'll be using a yoga strap for some of the poses but a scarf or a towel can work just as well but one of the moves I'll be using my folded jumper to support my lower back you might want to have one nearby for another move I'll be using some yoga blocks but books can do the trick just as well the video is one of a series that I've made with the MS Society as part of MS active together it's all about helping everyone with MS to be as active as we can if you haven't watched it already please watch my short introduction to the classes and make sure you're comfortable and safe before you start so we're going to start by getting in a comfortable position with our legs extended so just wiggle our bun we want our feet to be flexed up and we need to consider that you might have a foot drop I have mine on my right foot so we need to turn our feet around so they're fully straight and extend it and then first of all going to be checking in with ourselves so checking in with our heels our knees or hips chest and our crown of our head so legs are close together I'm going to start by warming up our shoulders feeling the gap between our head and our neck extending up and then we'll roll back the other way and then we're gently looking to the right and then gently coming back and just gently looking to the left so now we're going to start with our routine so start with we're gonna breathe in and look up with extending our fingers pushing our sit bones on the floor breathing in holding and breathing out into a forward bend so coming back to a rest position and then we'll do that again so when we are extending our arms we're thinking about pulling our finger you know middle finger right up and then we're pulling our shoulders down so we're getting a really good extension they're really feeling the stretch so breathing in and then breathing out as we go down to a forward bend so when we're thinking about forward bend we need to make sure that we're not curving our back too much so we're not trying as much as we can to touch our toes we want to be extending from the hips and then coming back down to it almost lifting our hips up and then extending down it doesn't matter if you don't touch your feet it's just go as far as that you need to go and actually you can use your strap if you feel a bit more comfortable with that sometimes I I do that so I'm keeping your legs a little bit further apart and then gently breathing in so we're reaching up and then forward bending down you should be able to tell if you are fully extended and that you're we're checking in with our back so the next position we're going to take our right foot over our left leg try and keep the palm of your foot down as much as possible and come in as much as possible as well to the inside of your leg this left foot is always flexed and then we're going to extend our spine up and then gently turning around to a spinal twist try and get your fingers as close as you can to your spine so breathing in here and as you're breathing out you're just looking over the corner of your show a little bit more and then coming back slowly into a neutral position don't forget to keep yourself hydrated throughout the class if you need to take a break at any time then just do that so extending the other leg so check in with your legs again and make sure that your feet are extended so we'll take a left foot over our right leg and then we're breathing in extending I fling up the shoulders and then gently coming back over looking over my shoulder so we want to take a couple of deep breaths in here so never hold your breath always breathe through the stretch checking with the toes up there flex will take a deep breath in and then see if you can look over a little bit further opening up the shoulder before you come back down gently coming out on the spinal stretch and then wiccaning the feet and then come back to a neutral position so now the next position that we're going back into the forward bend taking some deep breaths in here and breathing out and then this time we're going to curl up suit look at your tummy button and your curling up as you're reaching up slowly to a seated position so a good way of checking that you're in a good position on your sit bones is just lift them up and then as you place them down you can feel them really nestled in there and we're taking our left leg coming to the inside of our leg keeping our feet flexed reaching up and then heading down towards our big toe if you need to grab a scarf or a tea towel to use a strap loop it round your right foot hold on to it with both hands then with this make sure that our shoulders are relaxed and your back isn't arching forwards bend at the hips it's not about how far you can reach forward gently come back up and we'll do it on the other side so we put the soles of our right foot against the inside of our left leg reaching up breathing in shoulders are coming down and then the forward bending over our big toe and then looking down like I said taking the foot and we're taking it on the ball of our foot not the heel flexing the feet in and you should really feel like you're sitting down on your buttocks so take a couple of deep breaths in here relaxing the stomach and never feel like you're stretching and tensing just relax and breathe out breathes out through the stretch and then just rolling up gently up to a seated position so now we're going to go into our lying down part of our routine so if you just want to get yourself comfortable give yourself a wiggle move your legs side to side maybe your knees and your hips just how you feel comfortable maybe give them a bit of a and wiggle and so first of all we're gonna start with extending our legs so this is perfect for taking your belt or scarf we loop it around our right foot around the ball of effort not the heel hold your strap in both hands and flex your feet our other leg stays on the floor relax the shoulders and keep your hips and your head on the floor take your right leg up as far as you feel comfortable and make sure your big toe is stretching out breathe while we hold the pose and slowly lower your right leg then we do the same with the left leg we loop it around our left foot around the ball of our foots not the heel bring your left knee towards your chest and straighten your leg push your heel towards the ceiling the other leg stays on the floor and relax the shoulders keep your hips and your head on the floor take your leg up as far as feels comfortable and make sure your big toe is stretching out breathe as you hold the pose slowly lower your leg to the floor next we'll go into our bridge pose so we're going to check in with our bodies again so we're heel toe to the mat with our legs firmly placed on the mat legs are hip width distance apart and palms are facing down so we can grab a jumper if we need to with the small of our back having a slight bit of padding whichever you feel comfortable if you want to go extended you're pulling your stomach up a little bit and pushing your fingers flat on the mat and making sure that our legs are in so we're pushing down on our big toe and then if we want an extension a bit more then we can have our hands clasp together interlace the fingers rise up even further with your stomach and your hips with this extra stretch your back will arch very gently come back down move your hands from under you as you do hug your knees to your chest rocking left to right massaging the lower back you can do this in circles or you can go up and down just however you're feeling and stretch and then cross it like save the other way full stretch and benefits and now we're going into a spinal twist I use a block when I do this so I don't strain myself we bring our right knee up towards our chest and take a deep breath in slowly lower the right knee towards the floor on your left and rotate your head to look the other way look at your right hand put the block under your knee if it helps to support it breathe and then come back to the middle and we'll do it the other way bring your left knee up towards the chest then slowly lower the knee towards the floor on your right use the block if you need it and rotate your head to look the other way so that you're looking at your left hand breathe then come back to the middle shavasana is our last pose which is our resting pose so we're taking both legs straight out they're relaxed and open shoulders are open and our palms are facing upwards and our eyes are gently closed I'm gonna breathing deeply in through the nose and through the nose time and again just checking into your body thinking about the touch points on the mat say your feet the arms your head and give yourself thanks for the practice and have a good rest of the day namaste you can find all the videos online at MS Society org UK /ms active together
